QuestionText: Which element found in CFC and HCFC refrigerants is responsible for destroying stratospheric ozone?
OptionB: Chlorine
Explanation: Chlorine is the ozone killer. When UV light breaks a CFC molecule apart in the stratosphere, the freed chlorine atom destroys ozone molecules over and over — one chlorine atom can take out thousands of them. Fluorine is the tempting wrong answer because it's also in the molecule, but fluorine does not deplete ozone.

QuestionText: What does the ozone layer in the stratosphere do for life on Earth?
OptionA: Traps heat to keep the planet warm
OptionB: Produces the oxygen we breathe
Explanation: Stratospheric ozone acts as a sunscreen for the planet, absorbing harmful UV radiation before it reaches the surface. Less ozone means more UV, which increases skin cancer, cataracts, and crop damage. Trapping heat describes the greenhouse effect — a different problem.

QuestionText: Which list ranks refrigerant classes from HIGHEST to LOWEST ozone depletion potential (ODP)?
OptionB: CFC, HCFC, HFC
Explanation: CFCs are the worst (highest ODP), HCFCs are milder, and HFCs have zero ODP. The pattern to remember: the more chlorine and the longer the molecule survives, the higher the ODP. HFCs contain no chlorine at all, so they can't deplete ozone.

QuestionText: Why do HCFC refrigerants like R-22 have a lower ozone depletion potential than CFCs like R-12?
OptionB: The hydrogen in HCFCs makes them break down in the lower atmosphere before most reach the stratosphere
OptionC: HCFCs are heavier than air so they never rise
OptionD: HCFCs are only used in small quantities
Explanation: The hydrogen atom in an HCFC makes the molecule less stable, so most of it breaks apart in the lower atmosphere before it can carry chlorine up to the ozone layer. HCFCs DO contain chlorine — that's why their ODP is low but not zero. Weight has nothing to do with it; atmospheric mixing carries these gases upward regardless.

QuestionText: The ozone depletion potential (ODP) scale is defined by assigning a value of 1.0 to which refrigerant?
Explanation: CFC-11 (R-11) is the reference point — its ODP is defined as 1.0, and every other refrigerant is measured against it. R-12 also scores 1.0, but R-11 is the defined baseline. R-134a and R-410A are HFCs with an ODP of zero.

QuestionText: Under Section 608 of the Clean Air Act, knowingly venting CFC and HCFC refrigerants during service has been illegal since what date?
OptionB: November 15, 1995
Explanation: July 1, 1992 is the date venting of CFCs and HCFCs became illegal. November 15, 1995 is the tempting wrong answer — that's when the venting ban was extended to SUBSTITUTE refrigerants like HFCs. Keep the two dates straight: 1992 for the originals, 1995 for the substitutes.

QuestionText: Venting substitute refrigerants (such as HFC-134a and R-410A) has been prohibited since what date?
OptionB: November 14, 1994
Explanation: November 15, 1995 is when the venting prohibition was extended to substitutes such as HFCs. July 1, 1992 covers CFCs and HCFCs only. Even though HFCs don't harm the ozone layer, venting them is still illegal — they are powerful greenhouse gases.

QuestionText: Which of the following releases of refrigerant is NOT a violation of the Clean Air Act?
OptionA: Venting a system to the air to save recovery time
OptionB: The small puff released when disconnecting properly designed low-loss fittings after recovery
OptionC: Releasing refrigerant because the recovery machine is broken
OptionD: Blowing the charge out with nitrogen without recovering first
Explanation: Tiny releases that happen while making a good-faith effort to recover — like the trace amount in a low-loss fitting — are called 'de minimis' releases and are legal. Everything else listed is straight-up venting. A broken recovery machine is never an excuse; you must recover before opening a system.

QuestionText: The EPA may pay an award of up to how much for information that leads to penalizing someone who illegally vents refrigerant?
Explanation: The Clean Air Act allows the EPA to pay up to $10,000 to people who supply information leading to a penalty against a venting violator. Separate from the award, violators themselves face fines that can run tens of thousands of dollars per day, per violation — and technicians can lose their certification.

QuestionText: Who is legally allowed to purchase CFC and HCFC refrigerants like R-22?
OptionB: Anyone who owns air conditioning equipment
OptionC: EPA Section 608 certified technicians (or their employers)
Explanation: The sales restriction limits the purchase of ozone-depleting refrigerants to technicians certified under Section 608 (or 609 for motor vehicle A/C). A homeowner can own equipment containing refrigerant but can't walk into a supply house and buy a jug of R-22. This is one reason your certification card matters on the job.

QuestionText: Which EPA Section 608 certification type is required to service SMALL APPLIANCES like household refrigerators and window air conditioners?
OptionB: Type II
Explanation: Type I covers small appliances, Type II covers high-pressure equipment, and Type III covers low-pressure equipment (big chillers). Universal certification covers all three, but it is not the only way to work on small appliances — Type I alone is enough.

QuestionText: If EPA refrigerant regulations and your state or local regulations conflict, which do you follow?
OptionB: Always the state rule
OptionD: Whichever rule was written most recently
Explanation: You must comply with the stricter requirement. Federal EPA rules are the floor, not the ceiling — states and localities are allowed to pass tougher rules, and if they do, those tougher rules apply to you.

QuestionText: What is the Montreal Protocol?
OptionA: A US law that created technician certification
OptionB: An international treaty to phase out the production of ozone-depleting substances
OptionD: An ASHRAE safety standard for equipment rooms
Explanation: The Montreal Protocol is the international agreement (signed in 1987) committing countries to phase out ozone-depleting substances like CFCs and HCFCs. In the United States, it is implemented through the Clean Air Act — which is the US law, not the treaty itself.

QuestionText: Under the US phase-out schedule, production and import of CFC refrigerants such as R-12 ended in what year?
Explanation: CFC production and import for US consumption ended January 1, 1996. Any R-12 used after that comes from stockpiles or reclaimed refrigerant. 2010 and 2020 relate to later HCFC phase-out milestones (like R-22), which is why they're tempting.

QuestionText: R-22 belongs to which class of refrigerants?
OptionB: HCFC
Explanation: R-22 is an HCFC — it contains hydrogen, chlorine, fluorine, and carbon. Because it still contains chlorine it has a small but real ozone depletion potential, which is why it was phased out. R-12 is the classic CFC; R-134a and R-410A are HFCs.

QuestionText: Refrigerant blends in the 400 series (such as R-407C) are classified as what?
OptionA: Azeotropic blends that act like a single refrigerant
OptionB: Zeotropic blends that have a temperature glide
Explanation: 400-series blends are zeotropic: their components boil and condense at slightly different temperatures, creating a 'temperature glide.' 500-series blends (like R-502) are azeotropic and behave like a single compound. The series number is the giveaway on the exam.

QuestionText: Why must zeotropic blends like R-410A be charged into a system as a LIQUID?
OptionA: Liquid charging is faster and that is the only reason
OptionB: Charging vapor could fractionate the blend and change its composition
OptionC: Liquid refrigerant is easier to weigh
OptionD: Vapor charging would overheat the compressor
Explanation: In a blend, the components evaporate at different rates. If you draw vapor off the cylinder, you pull out more of the lighter component and leave the rest behind — that's fractionation, and it changes the blend's makeup and performance. Charging liquid (throttled carefully into the low side) keeps the mixture correct.

QuestionText: What is 'fractionation' in a refrigerant blend?
OptionA: The blend breaking down into acid when overheated
OptionB: Components of the blend leaking or evaporating at different rates, changing the blend's composition
OptionC: The refrigerant freezing into layers inside the cylinder
OptionD: Mixing two different refrigerants in one system
Explanation: Fractionation means the ingredients of a blend separate because they evaporate at different rates — during a vapor leak or vapor charging, the lighter component leaves faster. That's why a system with a zeotropic blend that has leaked significantly is often recovered and recharged with virgin refrigerant instead of just topped off.

QuestionText: A 500-series blend such as R-502 is azeotropic. What does that mean in practice?
OptionA: It behaves like a single-component refrigerant with one boiling point at a given pressure
OptionB: It must never be recovered
OptionC: It always has a large temperature glide
Explanation: Azeotropic blends act like one pure refrigerant: at a given pressure they boil and condense at a single temperature, with no glide and no meaningful fractionation. That's the opposite of zeotropic 400-series blends, which is what makes this a favorite exam contrast.

QuestionText: Which lubricant is used with HFC refrigerants like R-410A and R-134a?
OptionB: Polyolester (POE) oil
Explanation: HFC refrigerants don't mix well with the old mineral oils, so systems using them run polyolester (POE) synthetic oil. This matters on retrofits: converting an R-12 system to R-134a means changing the mineral oil to ester oil. POE oil also absorbs moisture aggressively, so keep it sealed.

QuestionText: What does it mean to RECOVER refrigerant?
OptionA: To clean refrigerant using filter-driers for reuse
OptionB: To remove refrigerant in any condition from a system and store it in an external container, without testing or processing it
OptionC: To re-process refrigerant to brand-new purity verified by lab analysis
Explanation: Recovery is simply getting the refrigerant out of the system and into an approved container — no cleaning, no testing. Cleaning it with oil separators and filter-driers is RECYCLING; processing it back to new-product purity with lab verification is RECLAIMING. The three R's are defined by how much processing happens.

QuestionText: What does it mean to RECYCLE refrigerant?
OptionA: To store it in any available container
OptionB: To vent it slowly through a filter
OptionC: To clean it for reuse by oil separation and single or multiple passes through filter-driers
OptionD: To send it to a chemical plant for destruction
Explanation: Recycling cleans the refrigerant on site or at a shop using oil separation and filter-driers, typically so it can go back into equipment belonging to the same owner. It does NOT certify purity — that's reclamation, which requires meeting the AHRI 700 standard verified by chemical analysis.

QuestionText: Before recovered refrigerant can be SOLD to a different owner, what must happen to it?
OptionA: It must be recycled with a filter-drier
OptionB: It must be reclaimed to AHRI 700 purity, verified by chemical analysis
OptionC: It must sit in storage for 30 days
OptionD: Nothing — recovered refrigerant can be resold as-is
Explanation: Refrigerant that changes ownership must be reclaimed — processed back to the AHRI 700 purity standard and verified by lab analysis. Recycled refrigerant can generally only go back into equipment of the same owner. This protects buyers from contaminated refrigerant destroying their equipment.

QuestionText: Reclaimed refrigerant must meet which industry purity standard?
OptionB: AHRI 700
Explanation: AHRI 700 is the purity standard for reclaimed refrigerant. ASHRAE 15 is the safety standard for equipment rooms, and DOT 39 relates to disposable cylinders — both are real standards used as decoys because you'll see them elsewhere on the exam.

QuestionText: What is the standard color scheme for a refrigerant RECOVERY cylinder?
OptionB: Green body with a red top
OptionC: Gray body with a yellow top
Explanation: Recovery cylinders are gray with yellow tops (shoulders). This tells everyone downstream the cylinder holds used, recovered refrigerant rather than virgin product. Cylinders must also be DOT-approved and clearly labeled with the refrigerant type.

QuestionText: A recovery cylinder should never be filled beyond what percentage of its capacity?
Explanation: 80% is the maximum safe fill level. The remaining space allows the liquid to expand as temperature rises. A cylinder filled too full can become 'hydrostatically' full of liquid with no vapor space — then even mild warming builds explosive pressure. Weigh cylinders during recovery; don't guess.

QuestionText: What should you do with a disposable (one-trip) refrigerant cylinder once it is empty?
OptionA: Refill it with recovered refrigerant since it held the same type
OptionB: Recover any remaining refrigerant, mark it empty, and recycle the metal — never refill it
OptionC: Vent the remaining vapor and throw it in the trash
OptionD: Use it as a compressed air tank in the shop
Explanation: Disposable cylinders are built for one trip only — refilling one is illegal and dangerous because the cylinder and its valve aren't designed for reuse. Before discarding, recover the remaining vapor (a 'empty' disposable can still hold a surprising amount), then render it useless and recycle the steel.

QuestionText: A large refrigerant leak happens in an enclosed equipment room. Why is this immediately dangerous to anyone inside?
OptionA: Refrigerant vapor is flammable in any concentration
OptionB: Refrigerant vapor is heavier than air and displaces the oxygen you need to breathe
OptionC: Refrigerant vapor causes instant frostbite through clothing
OptionD: The leak will always trigger an explosion
Explanation: Most refrigerant vapors are heavier than air, so they pool in low spots and enclosed spaces and push the breathable air out — people have died from oxygen deprivation this way. That's why you evacuate, ventilate, and use a self-contained breathing apparatus (SCBA) for major leaks. Most common refrigerants aren't flammable; suffocation is the killer.

QuestionText: Which gas should be used to pressurize a system for leak testing, and with what safeguards?
OptionB: Compressed shop air, straight from the compressor
OptionC: Dry nitrogen, with a pressure regulator and a pressure relief valve in the line
Explanation: Dry nitrogen is the only safe choice, and it must go through a pressure regulator with a relief valve so you can't over-pressurize the system. Oxygen or compressed air can explode when they contact refrigerant oil under pressure — that combination has killed technicians. This is one of the most-repeated safety questions on the exam.

QuestionText: What happens when R-22 vapor is drawn across an open flame or a glowing hot surface?
OptionB: It decomposes into toxic gases such as hydrochloric and hydrofluoric acid vapors
OptionD: It instantly freezes the flame source
Explanation: Heat breaks refrigerant molecules apart into nasty products — hydrochloric acid vapor, hydrofluoric acid vapor, and potentially phosgene gas. That sharp, biting smell near a leak and a flame is your warning. This is why you never braze on a system that still contains refrigerant and why halide torch detectors require ventilation.

QuestionText: Why must moisture be removed from a refrigeration system, and what removes it?
OptionA: Moisture improves oil flow; add it deliberately
OptionB: Moisture reacts with refrigerant and oil to form acids and sludge; a deep vacuum (evacuation) boils it out
OptionC: Moisture only matters in low-pressure systems; drain it with a valve
OptionD: Moisture freezes the compressor windings; remove it with compressed air
Explanation: Water plus refrigerant plus heat makes acid, which chews up motor windings and metal and creates sludge. Pulling a deep vacuum lowers the boiling point of water so it boils off and gets pumped out. Blowing compressed air through a system would ADD moisture — air always carries water vapor.

QuestionText: When shipping a filled refrigerant recovery cylinder, which requirement applies?
OptionA: Any sturdy box with padding is acceptable
OptionB: The cylinder must be DOT-approved and labeled/marked in accordance with DOT hazardous materials regulations
OptionC: Cylinders can only be shipped completely full
OptionD: Refrigerant cylinders may never be transported on public roads
Explanation: Transporting refrigerant falls under Department of Transportation (DOT) hazardous materials rules: approved cylinders, proper labels identifying the contents, and secure upright transport. Cylinders should be at no more than 80% capacity — never 'completely full' — and yes, they move on roads every day, legally, when packaged right.

QuestionText: Under EPA rules, a 'small appliance' is a product that is fully manufactured, charged, and hermetically sealed at the factory with how much refrigerant or less?
OptionB: 15 pounds
Explanation: A small appliance contains 5 pounds of refrigerant or less AND is charged and hermetically sealed at the factory. Both parts matter: a field-charged system isn't a small appliance no matter how little refrigerant it holds. 50 and 200 pounds are thresholds you'll see in Type II rules, which is why they're used as decoys.

QuestionText: Which of the following is considered a small appliance?
OptionB: A window air conditioning unit
Explanation: Window units, household refrigerators and freezers, dehumidifiers, PTACs, and vending machines are the classic small appliances — factory-sealed with 5 pounds or less. A split system is charged in the field and typically holds more than 5 pounds, so it falls under Type II even when it serves a house.

QuestionText: Which of the following is NOT a small appliance?
OptionB: A packaged terminal air conditioner (PTAC)
OptionC: A residential central split-system condensing unit
Explanation: A central split system is assembled and charged in the field, so it fails the 'hermetically sealed at the factory' test — and it usually holds more than 5 pounds anyway. Dehumidifiers, PTACs, and vending machines are all factory-sealed products within the 5-pound limit.

QuestionText: Recovery equipment manufactured after November 15, 1993 must recover what percentage of the refrigerant from a small appliance when its compressor IS operating?
Explanation: 90% with an operating compressor. If the compressor is dead, the requirement drops to 80% — because you can't use the system's own pressure to help push refrigerant out. Either way, achieving a 4 inches Hg vacuum also satisfies the rule.

QuestionText: When a small appliance's compressor is NOT operating, recovery equipment (made after Nov 15, 1993) must recover what percentage of the charge?
Explanation: 80% when the compressor won't run, versus 90% when it will. Older recovery equipment (made before November 15, 1993) only has to hit 80% in both cases. Nobody can recover 100% — some refrigerant always stays dissolved in the oil.

QuestionText: Instead of meeting the percentage requirement, recovery from a small appliance is also considered complete when the system is evacuated to what vacuum level?
OptionB: 10 inches Hg vacuum
Explanation: 4 inches of mercury (Hg) vacuum is the alternative standard for small appliances. The deeper levels — 10 and 15 inches — belong to Type II high-pressure equipment, and 25 mm Hg absolute belongs to Type III low-pressure equipment. Matching the vacuum level to the equipment type is a guaranteed exam topic.

QuestionText: Recovery equipment used on small appliances must be certified by whom?
OptionB: An EPA-approved testing organization (tested to the AHRI 740 standard)
Explanation: Recovery machines must be tested and certified by an EPA-approved laboratory to the AHRI 740 standard. OSHA governs worker safety, not equipment certification, and building departments deal with codes and permits — common-sense-sounding decoys that are wrong here.

QuestionText: Businesses that service small appliances must do what regarding their recovery equipment?
OptionA: Nothing — small appliance work is exempt from equipment rules
OptionB: Certify to the EPA (by signed statement to their EPA Regional Office) that they have acquired certified recovery equipment
OptionC: Have their equipment inspected by the EPA annually
OptionD: Lease equipment only from EPA-owned depots
Explanation: Shops must register with the EPA by sending a signed statement listing the certified recovery equipment they've acquired. There's no annual EPA inspection program and no exemption — the paperwork requirement is the piece people forget.

QuestionText: What is 'system-dependent' (passive) recovery?
OptionA: Recovery that uses the appliance's own compressor or internal pressure to move refrigerant into a non-pressurized container
OptionB: Recovery using a machine with its own built-in compressor
OptionC: Letting the refrigerant vent slowly through a filter
OptionD: Any recovery that takes more than one hour
Explanation: System-dependent (passive) recovery relies on the appliance itself — its compressor or its internal pressure — to push refrigerant into a recovery container, which may be non-pressurized. Self-contained (active) recovery uses a machine with its own pump or compressor. Venting through a filter is illegal, full stop.

QuestionText: What makes recovery equipment 'self-contained' (active)?
OptionA: It fits in one carrying case
OptionB: It has its own compressor or pump to draw out the refrigerant
OptionD: It only works on operating compressors
Explanation: Self-contained means the recovery unit brings its own muscle — a built-in compressor or pump — so it doesn't depend on the appliance's compressor at all. That's exactly why it works on dead systems where passive recovery struggles.

QuestionText: When using system-dependent recovery on a small appliance whose compressor does NOT run, what should you do?
OptionA: Recover from the low side only
OptionB: Access BOTH the high side and the low side of the system
OptionC: Skip recovery since the compressor is dead
OptionD: Pressurize the system with nitrogen first
Explanation: With a dead compressor there's nothing circulating the refrigerant, so you need access valves on both the high and low sides to capture the full charge. Recovery is never optional — a dead compressor doesn't create a venting exemption. Nitrogen would just contaminate the recovered refrigerant.

QuestionText: Which trick helps release refrigerant trapped in the oil of a small appliance's non-operating compressor during recovery?
OptionA: Gently heating and tapping the compressor
OptionB: Packing the compressor in ice
Explanation: Refrigerant dissolves in compressor oil. Warming the compressor (and tapping it lightly with a rubber mallet) drives that dissolved refrigerant out of the oil so it can be recovered. Cooling it does the opposite — cold oil holds MORE refrigerant.

QuestionText: Why shouldn't a solderless, clamp-on piercing access valve be left permanently installed on a small appliance?
OptionB: These valves tend to leak over time
OptionC: It makes the appliance run louder
OptionD: EPA rules require removing all valves within one hour
Explanation: Clamp-on piercing valves are a quick way in, but their gasket seal degrades and they leak with time — so they should be removed after service (or replaced with a brazed-in valve) and always leak-tested while installed. There's no one-hour EPA rule; the issue is purely that they leak.

QuestionText: Why should you never recover different refrigerants into the same cylinder?
OptionB: Mixed refrigerants may be impossible to reclaim and often must be destroyed at your cost
OptionC: Mixing always causes an immediate explosion
OptionD: It is fine as long as both are HFCs
Explanation: A mixed-refrigerant cylinder usually can't be separated back into usable products — reclaimers will reject it, and you pay for destruction. It's rarely an instant safety catastrophe; it's an expensive, avoidable waste. Identify the refrigerant before recovery and dedicate cylinders accordingly.

QuestionText: Before recovering from a small appliance, how do you identify which refrigerant it contains?
OptionA: By the color of the cabinet
OptionB: Check the nameplate/label, and verify with a pressure-temperature comparison if in doubt
Explanation: The nameplate or model label states the refrigerant and charge. If the label is gone or a retrofit is suspected, compare the system's standing pressure to the saturation pressure for that refrigerant at the current temperature. Household refrigerators alone have used R-12, R-134a, and R-600a over the years — never assume.

QuestionText: Recovery from a small appliance is going very slowly on a cold day. What is the most likely reason?
OptionA: Low ambient temperature keeps system pressure low, slowing refrigerant flow to the recovery device
OptionB: Cold weather makes refrigerant heavier
Explanation: Refrigerant pressure follows temperature. On a cold day the system's pressure is low, so there's less push driving refrigerant into your recovery setup. Warming the appliance (safely) speeds things up. Common refrigerants don't freeze at any temperature you'll ever work in.

QuestionText: Recovery equipment manufactured after November 15, 1993 must be equipped with what feature to minimize refrigerant release when hoses are disconnected?
OptionB: Low-loss fittings
Explanation: Low-loss fittings close themselves off when disconnected, trapping the refrigerant in the hose instead of releasing it. The tiny puff that still escapes is a legal 'de minimis' release. An 'automatic venting port' is the opposite of the goal — venting is what we're preventing.

QuestionText: Who is responsible for making sure refrigerant is recovered from a refrigerator before it is scrapped and recycled?
OptionB: The original manufacturer
OptionC: The final person in the disposal chain (such as the scrap yard), unless they have a signed statement that the refrigerant was already recovered
Explanation: The last business in the disposal chain must ensure recovery happened — either by doing it or by obtaining a signed statement (with name, address, and date) from whoever delivered the appliance saying they recovered the refrigerant. Small appliances are exempt from leak-repair rules, but never from recovery-before-disposal.

QuestionText: Refrigerant recovered from a customer's small appliance may be returned WITHOUT reclamation to which equipment?
OptionB: Equipment belonging to the same owner
OptionC: Only the exact appliance it came from
OptionD: It must always be reclaimed before any reuse
Explanation: Recovered (or recycled) refrigerant can go back into the same owner's equipment without lab-certified reclamation — it doesn't have to be the identical appliance. The AHRI 700 reclamation requirement kicks in when the refrigerant changes ownership.

QuestionText: Older household refrigerators used R-12. What refrigerants are common in newer household refrigerators?
OptionB: R-22 or R-11
Explanation: R-134a replaced R-12 in domestic refrigeration through the 1990s and 2000s, and many current models use R-600a (isobutane), a flammable hydrocarbon. R-410A is a residential air conditioning refrigerant, and R-11 belongs to big low-pressure chillers — different worlds.

QuestionText: What special hazard does R-600a (isobutane) in a modern refrigerator present during service?
OptionA: It is extremely toxic to touch
OptionB: It is flammable — keep ignition sources away and use tools rated for flammable refrigerants
OptionC: It operates at dangerously high pressure
OptionD: It destroys ozone faster than CFCs
Explanation: Isobutane is a hydrocarbon — think lighter fluid. A leak near a spark or open flame can ignite, so service requires ventilation, spark-free tools, and no brazing until the charge is out and the system is purged. It's actually a low-pressure, ozone-safe refrigerant; flammability is the whole story.

QuestionText: When retrofitting an old R-12 refrigerator to R-134a, which change is required?
OptionA: No changes — just top off the R-12 with R-134a
OptionB: Replace the mineral oil with ester (POE) oil and replace the filter-drier
OptionD: Paint the cylinder gray and yellow
Explanation: R-134a will not mix with the mineral oil that R-12 systems use, so the oil must be changed to polyolester and the filter-drier replaced with one compatible with R-134a. Topping off R-12 with R-134a creates an illegal, unreclaimable mixed refrigerant — never mix refrigerants in a system.

QuestionText: What personal protective equipment is the minimum when transferring refrigerant?
OptionA: Safety glasses and gloves — liquid refrigerant can cause frostbite
OptionB: A full hazmat suit at all times
OptionD: None is needed for small appliances
Explanation: Liquid refrigerant boils at skin temperature and flash-freezes tissue on contact — frostbite injuries to eyes and hands are the everyday risk. Safety glasses and gloves are the baseline for any transfer. A full hazmat suit isn't the standard for routine work; eyes and skin protection are.

QuestionText: Refrigerant recovered from a small appliance must be stored in what kind of container?
OptionA: Any clean metal can with a lid
OptionB: A DOT-approved recovery cylinder (or a non-pressurized container when using system-dependent recovery)
OptionD: The original disposable cylinder the refrigerant came in
Explanation: Pressurized storage requires a DOT-approved recovery cylinder, filled to no more than 80%. The one exception: passive, system-dependent recovery may capture the charge in an approved non-pressurized container. Refilling a disposable cylinder is illegal and dangerous.

QuestionText: You evacuate a small appliance to 4 inches Hg vacuum and stop. A few minutes later the pressure has risen noticeably. What does this most likely mean?
OptionA: The vacuum gauge is always wrong
OptionB: Refrigerant trapped in the compressor oil is boiling off (or the system has a leak) — resume recovery
OptionC: The recovery is complete and the rise is normal air expansion
Explanation: A pressure rebound after evacuation means more refrigerant is coming out of the oil — recover again until the system holds its vacuum. It could also indicate a leak pulling in air. Either way, walking away at the first sign of 4 inches Hg without confirming it holds is how techs fail the intent of the rule.

QuestionText: Small appliances are exempt from the EPA's leak repair requirements. Does that mean venting from a leaky refrigerator is legal?
OptionB: No — the venting prohibition still applies to all appliances; only the mandatory leak-REPAIR thresholds don't apply
OptionC: Yes, but only for appliances older than 20 years
OptionD: Only if the leak is smaller than one ounce per year
Explanation: The leak-repair rules (the 10%/20%/30% thresholds) apply to appliances with 50+ pounds of charge, so small appliances are outside them. But knowingly venting refrigerant is illegal on ANY appliance. Exempt from repair deadlines does not mean free to release.

QuestionText: Why can't you use a plain vacuum pump by itself as recovery equipment?
OptionB: A vacuum pump discharges to the atmosphere — using one to remove refrigerant is venting
OptionC: Vacuum pumps only work on large systems
OptionD: Vacuum pump oil dissolves refrigerant permanently
Explanation: A vacuum pump's exhaust goes straight to the air. Pulling refrigerant out with one releases the charge — that's illegal venting, not recovery. Vacuum pumps come out AFTER recovery, to evacuate air and moisture from the empty system before recharge.

QuestionText: A technician holding ONLY Type I certification may work on which equipment?
OptionA: Any air conditioning system up to 5 tons
OptionB: Small appliances only
Explanation: Type I covers small appliances, period — household refrigerators, freezers, window units, PTACs, dehumidifiers, vending machines. A 5-ton split system is high-pressure Type II territory regardless of who owns it. To work across all categories you need Universal certification.

QuestionText: During system-dependent recovery of a small appliance, the refrigerant may legally be captured in which of the following?
OptionA: An approved non-pressurized container (such as a recovery bag)
OptionB: A soda bottle with a tight cap
Explanation: Passive recovery works at low pressure, so approved non-pressurized containers — including purpose-made recovery bags — are permitted for small appliances. Improvised containers are neither approved nor safe, and an open bucket is simply venting with extra steps.

QuestionText: When brazing a new access fitting onto a small appliance's tubing (after recovery), why should you flow a low pressure of dry nitrogen through the tubing?
OptionA: Nitrogen makes the solder joint shinier
OptionB: It prevents oxidation scale from forming inside the tubing, which would otherwise circulate and clog the system
OptionC: It is required by the DOT
OptionD: It cools the tubing so you can braze faster
Explanation: Brazing heat plus oxygen creates black oxide scale inside the pipe; flowing nitrogen displaces the oxygen so the inside stays clean. Scale that breaks loose plugs the cap tube and filter-drier — a slow death for a refrigerator. This is a shop-practice question that shows up because it protects the equipment.

QuestionText: A HIGH-PRESSURE appliance uses a refrigerant with a boiling point in what range at atmospheric pressure?
OptionB: Between -50°C and 10°C (-58°F and 50°F)
Explanation: High-pressure refrigerants boil between -50°C and 10°C at atmospheric pressure — that covers R-22, R-134a, R-410A, and R-407C. Boiling below -50°C makes it a VERY-high-pressure refrigerant (R-13, R-503), and boiling above 10°C makes it low-pressure (R-11, R-123), which is Type III territory.

QuestionText: Which refrigerants are classified as VERY-high-pressure?
OptionB: R-22 and R-410A
Explanation: R-13 and R-503 boil below -50°C at atmospheric pressure, putting them in the very-high-pressure class used in ultra-low-temperature work. R-22 and R-410A are ordinary high-pressure refrigerants, and R-11/R-123 are the low-pressure chiller refrigerants.

QuestionText: Using recovery equipment made after November 15, 1993, to what level must you evacuate an R-22 appliance holding LESS than 200 pounds before opening it?
OptionB: 10 inches Hg vacuum
Explanation: Small R-22 systems (under 200 pounds) only need to be brought down to atmospheric pressure — 0 inches Hg gauge. Go to 200 pounds or more of R-22 and the requirement deepens to 10 inches Hg vacuum. The 25 mm figure belongs to low-pressure (Type III) equipment.

QuestionText: An R-22 system contains 300 pounds of refrigerant. Using current recovery equipment, what evacuation level is required before disposal or opening?
OptionB: 4 inches Hg vacuum
Explanation: R-22 appliances with 200 pounds or more must be pulled to 10 inches Hg vacuum. The 15-inch requirement is for OTHER high-pressure refrigerants (like R-410A) at 200 pounds or more — R-22 gets a slightly easier target because it's harder to condense.

QuestionText: For high-pressure refrigerants OTHER than R-22 (such as R-410A), what are the required evacuation levels with current equipment?
OptionA: 0 inches Hg regardless of charge size
OptionB: 10 inches Hg under 200 lbs; 15 inches Hg at 200 lbs or more
OptionC: 15 inches Hg under 200 lbs; 25 inches Hg at 200 lbs or more
OptionD: 4 inches Hg regardless of charge size
Explanation: Non-R-22 high-pressure systems: 10 inches Hg vacuum below 200 pounds, 15 inches Hg at 200 pounds and above. Compare with R-22's easier levels (0 and 10 inches). The 4-inch level belongs to small appliances — different rule set entirely.

QuestionText: To what level must a VERY-high-pressure appliance (R-13, R-503) be evacuated?
OptionA: 0 inches Hg (atmospheric), regardless of charge size
OptionB: 10 inches Hg vacuum
Explanation: Very-high-pressure refrigerants only require evacuation to atmospheric pressure (0 inches Hg). Their extreme pressures make deep vacuum recovery impractical, so the rule stops at zero gauge. This is the easy-to-forget corner of the evacuation table.

QuestionText: You're recovering from a system with a known bad leak. Pulling the required vacuum would drag air and moisture in through the leak. What should you do?
OptionA: Pull the full vacuum anyway — rules are rules
OptionB: Evacuate the system to 0 psig (atmospheric pressure) instead
OptionC: Skip recovery entirely since the system leaks
OptionD: Pressurize the system with oxygen to find the leak first
Explanation: When leaks make the standard vacuum level impossible without contaminating the system, the EPA allows evacuating to atmospheric pressure (0 psig) instead. Skipping recovery is venting — illegal. And oxygen near refrigerant oil is an explosion hazard, never a leak-finding tool.

QuestionText: What is the fastest way to remove the bulk of the charge from a large high-pressure system?
OptionB: Recover liquid refrigerant first, then finish with vapor
OptionC: Heat the whole system with torches
OptionD: Open the system and let it equalize
Explanation: Liquid recovery moves pounds of refrigerant many times faster than vapor recovery — a pound of liquid occupies a tiny fraction of the space of a pound of vapor. Start at a liquid port (liquid line or king valve), then switch to vapor to finish the job.

QuestionText: From which point on the system should liquid refrigerant be removed during recovery?
OptionB: The suction line at the evaporator
OptionC: The liquid line or the king valve on the receiver outlet
Explanation: Liquid lives between the condenser/receiver and the metering device, so the liquid line — or the king valve on the receiver — is where you tap it. The suction line carries vapor; drawing 'liquid' there means the system is flooding, which is a malfunction, not a technique.

QuestionText: Recovery into your cylinder has slowed to a crawl because the cylinder has warmed up and its pressure has risen. What speeds the transfer back up?
OptionA: Cooling the recovery cylinder (ice bath or refrigeration)
OptionB: Heating the recovery cylinder
OptionC: Venting a little from the cylinder to drop its pressure
Explanation: Refrigerant flows from higher pressure to lower pressure. Cooling the cylinder condenses vapor inside it and drops its pressure, restoring flow. Heating does the opposite, and venting to relieve pressure is illegal. This is the practical physics the exam loves.

QuestionText: After evacuating a system to the required level, you watch the gauge and the pressure climbs again. What does this indicate?
OptionA: Refrigerant is boiling out of the compressor oil (or the system is leaking) — recovery isn't finished
OptionB: The system is now overcharged
OptionC: Normal behavior — vacuum always decays instantly
Explanation: A rising pressure after evacuation means refrigerant dissolved in the oil is still boiling off — or air is sneaking in through a leak. Either way, resume recovery and confirm the system HOLDS the required vacuum before opening it. A solid vacuum that holds is the actual finish line.

QuestionText: For appliances with 50 or more pounds of ozone-depleting refrigerant, what annual leak rates trigger mandatory repair?
OptionA: 10% comfort cooling, 20% commercial refrigeration, 30% industrial process refrigeration
OptionB: 35% for all equipment types
Explanation: The current trigger rates: 10% for comfort cooling, 20% for commercial refrigeration, 30% for industrial process refrigeration — calculated as a percentage of the full charge leaked per year. The old 35%/15% numbers were tightened in 2019, so beware of outdated study materials.

QuestionText: Once a leak above the threshold rate is discovered in a 50+ pound ODS appliance, the owner generally must have it repaired within how many days?
OptionB: 30 days
Explanation: 30 days to repair is the general rule (extensions exist for industrial shutdown constraints). The one-year figure is the deadline for COMPLETING a retrofit or retirement plan if the owner goes that route instead of repairing — a classic answer-swap trap.

QuestionText: Instead of repairing a chronically leaking appliance, the owner may develop a retrofit or retirement plan. What are the deadlines?
OptionA: Plan within 7 days, complete within 60 days
OptionB: Plan within 30 days, complete within 1 year
OptionC: Plan within 90 days, complete within 5 years
OptionD: No deadlines apply to retrofit plans
Explanation: The plan must be developed (and kept at the site) within 30 days, and the retrofit or retirement must be finished within one year. It's an alternative to repair, not an escape hatch — the clock runs either way.

QuestionText: After repairing a leak on a 50+ pound appliance, what testing is required to confirm the fix?
OptionA: No testing — a repair invoice is enough
OptionB: An initial verification test before adding refrigerant, and a follow-up verification test once the system is running
OptionC: Only a visual inspection after 30 days
Explanation: Two tests: an initial verification test (like a pressure or vacuum test) before the full charge goes back in, then a follow-up verification test with the system operating at normal conditions. The pair proves the leak is actually fixed, not just painted over.

QuestionText: How is an appliance's leak rate expressed under EPA rules?
OptionB: The percentage of the full charge lost per 12-month period (annualized)
OptionD: Parts per million in the equipment room air
Explanation: Leak rate = percentage of the appliance's full charge lost over a rolling 12-month period. If a 100-pound system needed 20 pounds added over the past year, that's a 20% annual leak rate — at the trigger point for commercial refrigeration.

QuestionText: What is the standard two-step approach for finding a refrigerant leak on a high-pressure system?
OptionA: Listen carefully, then feel for cold spots
OptionB: Use an electronic or ultrasonic detector to find the general area, then pinpoint with soap bubbles
OptionD: Pressurize with oxygen and watch for frost
Explanation: Electronic and ultrasonic detectors sniff out the neighborhood of the leak; a soap-bubble solution then pinpoints the exact spot. Adding refrigerant to a leaking system without repair wastes product and can violate leak-repair rules. Oxygen is never used — explosion risk with oil.

QuestionText: When pressure-testing a system with dry nitrogen, what pressure must you never exceed?
OptionB: 1,000 psig on any system
OptionC: Whatever the nitrogen cylinder can deliver
Explanation: The equipment nameplate lists design test pressures — never exceed the LOW-side test pressure, because the low side is the weakest link and the whole system sees your nitrogen. A nitrogen cylinder holds 2,000+ psig, which is why the regulator and relief valve aren't optional.

QuestionText: Air (a non-condensable gas) has gotten into a high-pressure system. Where does it collect and what symptom does it cause?
OptionA: In the evaporator, causing low suction pressure
OptionB: In the condenser, causing higher-than-normal head pressure
OptionC: In the oil, causing foaming only
OptionD: It dissolves harmlessly into the refrigerant
Explanation: Air can't condense at system temperatures, so it stacks up in the condenser, taking up condensing surface and driving head pressure above what the refrigerant's pressure-temperature relationship predicts. High head pressure means wasted energy and stressed equipment — the fix is recovery, evacuation, and recharge.

QuestionText: You recovered 40 pounds of R-410A from a customer's decommissioned rooftop unit. A different customer wants to buy it. What must happen first?
OptionA: Nothing — a sale between customers is private
OptionB: Filter it through one filter-drier
OptionC: It must be reclaimed to AHRI 700 purity by a certified reclaimer before changing ownership
OptionD: It must be mixed with virgin refrigerant 50/50
Explanation: Change of ownership triggers the reclamation requirement: processing to AHRI 700 purity with chemical analysis to prove it. A single filter pass is recycling — good enough only for reuse in the SAME owner's equipment. Selling un-reclaimed used refrigerant risks the buyer's equipment and your certification.

QuestionText: Recovery machines built after November 15, 1993 for high-pressure work must meet which requirements?
OptionA: Painted safety orange and weigh under 50 lbs
OptionB: Certified by an EPA-approved testing lab and equipped with low-loss fittings
OptionC: Capable of recovering 100% of any charge
OptionD: Built with a sight glass on every port
Explanation: The two legal must-haves: certification by an EPA-approved testing organization (per AHRI 740) and low-loss fittings that minimize release on disconnect. No machine recovers 100% — some refrigerant always remains dissolved in oil — and the rules say nothing about paint color.

QuestionText: What is the 'push-pull' recovery method used for?
OptionA: Removing air from a running system
OptionB: Transferring large volumes of LIQUID refrigerant by pulling vapor from the recovery cylinder and pushing it into the appliance, forcing liquid out into the cylinder
OptionC: Recovering vapor twice as fast with two machines
Explanation: In push-pull, the recovery machine pulls vapor off the top of the recovery cylinder and discharges it into the appliance, and that pressure pushes liquid refrigerant out of the appliance into the cylinder. It shines on systems holding large liquid volumes. It moves liquid — it does nothing about air.

QuestionText: Why must you avoid skin contact with the oil from a hermetic compressor that suffered an electrical burnout?
OptionB: Burnout oil contains acids formed when the motor burned — it can burn skin
OptionC: The oil is always boiling hot days later
OptionD: Oil stains are hard to wash out
Explanation: A motor burnout cooks the refrigerant and oil into acidic sludge. That acid attacks skin just like it attacks motor windings — wear gloves and eye protection when draining or handling it. Temperature isn't the issue after the fact; chemistry is.

QuestionText: After replacing a compressor that failed from a severe burnout, what should be installed to protect the new compressor?
OptionB: A suction-line filter-drier (and a new liquid-line drier) to catch acid and debris
OptionD: Nothing — the new compressor cleans the system itself
Explanation: Burnout leaves acid and carbon throughout the system. A suction-line filter-drier catches contaminants before they enter the new compressor, and the liquid-line drier gets replaced as a matter of course. Check and replace the suction drier as pressure drop develops. New compressors don't clean systems; they become victims of dirty ones.

QuestionText: After repairs, you evacuate a system for dehydration. What reading indicates the system is properly dehydrated?
OptionB: A micron gauge holds a deep vacuum (around 500 microns) with the pump isolated
OptionD: The nitrogen holds for one hour
Explanation: Dehydration is verified with a micron gauge: pull down to roughly 500 microns, isolate the pump, and confirm the vacuum holds. If it creeps up, there's still moisture boiling off or a leak. A compound gauge is far too coarse to prove dryness, and nitrogen tests find leaks, not moisture.

QuestionText: How should a zeotropic blend like R-407C be charged into a system?
OptionA: As vapor from the top of the cylinder
OptionB: As liquid (from the cylinder's liquid port), throttled into the system to avoid slugging
OptionC: Either way — it makes no difference
Explanation: Zeotropic blends must leave the cylinder as liquid so the mixture stays in its designed proportions — vapor charging fractionates the blend. Throttle the liquid carefully into the suction side (or charge into the liquid line with the system off) so liquid never slugs the compressor.

QuestionText: Which of these does Type II certification cover?
OptionB: Motor vehicle air conditioning
OptionC: Split-system air conditioners, heat pumps, and supermarket rack systems using high-pressure refrigerant
Explanation: Type II covers high-pressure and very-high-pressure appliances that aren't small appliances or motor vehicle A/C — splits, packaged rooftop units, heat pumps, market racks. Household refrigerators are Type I, R-123 chillers are Type III, and car A/C falls under Section 609, a separate certification.

QuestionText: Owners of appliances holding 50 or more pounds of refrigerant must keep what records?
OptionB: Records of refrigerant purchased and added during service, and leak inspections/servicing history
Explanation: Servicing records — especially the amount of refrigerant added, which is how leak rates get calculated — plus leak inspection records must be maintained. If refrigerant additions aren't documented, there's no way to prove the appliance is under its leak-rate threshold.

QuestionText: Why should you never energize a system's compressor while the system is under a deep vacuum?
OptionB: The motor windings can arc and be destroyed — a vacuum is a poor electrical insulator
Explanation: In a deep vacuum there are too few gas molecules to resist electrical discharge, so energized motor windings can arc across the gap and burn out instantly. Complete your evacuation, break the vacuum with refrigerant charge, and only then run the compressor.

QuestionText: What must you check about a recovery cylinder before recovering R-410A into it?
OptionB: That it is rated for R-410A's higher pressure and dedicated to the correct refrigerant, with a current test date
OptionC: That it is completely full already
OptionD: That it once held R-22, which is close enough
Explanation: R-410A runs roughly 50–60% higher pressure than R-22, so the cylinder must carry a pressure rating suitable for it (DOT-rated for the service), be within its hydrostatic test date, and be dedicated to R-410A to prevent mixing. Putting R-410A into a cylinder holding R-22 residue creates an unreclaimable mixture.

QuestionText: A LOW-PRESSURE appliance uses a refrigerant whose boiling point at atmospheric pressure is:
OptionB: Between -50°C and 10°C
Explanation: Low-pressure refrigerants — R-11, R-123, R-113 — boil ABOVE 10°C (50°F) at atmospheric pressure. That's why these machines run below atmospheric pressure in normal operation. Boiling between -50°C and 10°C defines high-pressure (Type II) refrigerants.

QuestionText: At normal operating conditions, the evaporator of a low-pressure centrifugal chiller runs at what pressure?
OptionB: Below atmospheric pressure — in a vacuum
Explanation: Low-pressure chillers operate in a vacuum. An R-123 machine's evaporator typically runs around 10 inches Hg vacuum or deeper. This single fact drives most of the Type III rules: leaks pull air IN, purge units exist, and test pressures are tiny.

QuestionText: Because a low-pressure chiller operates in a vacuum, what does a leak do?
OptionB: Lets air and moisture leak INTO the machine
OptionD: Releases refrigerant vapor at high velocity
Explanation: With the inside pressure below atmospheric, a leak flows backwards compared to a high-pressure system: air and moisture get sucked in. That contaminates the refrigerant, raises head pressure, and forms acids — which is exactly why these machines carry purge units.

QuestionText: What is the job of the purge unit on a low-pressure chiller?
OptionB: To remove air and other non-condensable gases that accumulate in the machine
Explanation: The purge unit collects and expels the air (non-condensables) that leaks into a machine running in a vacuum, while returning as much refrigerant as possible. Without it, accumulated air would drive condensing pressure up and efficiency down.

QuestionText: Where do non-condensable gases (air) collect in a low-pressure chiller, and where does the purge unit draw from?
OptionB: The oil sump
Explanation: Air rises with the refrigerant vapor but can't condense, so it accumulates at the top of the condenser — and that's exactly where the purge unit taps in. Knowing WHERE air collects is the memorable half of this exam question.

QuestionText: A chiller's purge unit has started running far more often than usual. What does this indicate?
OptionA: The machine has developed a leak letting air in — find and repair it
OptionB: The refrigerant is wearing out
OptionD: The chilled water is too cold
Explanation: Excessive purge operation means excessive air, and air gets in through leaks. The purge unit is treating the symptom; the machine needs a leak check and repair. Refrigerant doesn't 'wear out,' and season alone doesn't put air inside a sealed machine.

QuestionText: The rupture disc on a low-pressure chiller is designed to relieve at what pressure?
OptionB: 15 psig
Explanation: 15 psig is the rupture disc setting on low-pressure machines. These vessels are not built for high pressure — they live in a vacuum — so the relief point is startlingly low compared to Type II equipment. It pairs with the 10 psig leak-test limit: test below the disc's setting.

QuestionText: What is the MAXIMUM pressure allowed when leak-testing a low-pressure chiller?
OptionB: 15 psig
Explanation: Never exceed 10 psig. The rupture disc lets go at 15 psig, so 10 psig keeps a safety margin — blow the disc and you dump the machine's charge and buy a repair. High-pressure habits (100+ psig nitrogen tests) will destroy a low-pressure machine.

QuestionText: What is the recommended way to raise a low-pressure chiller's pressure to 10 psig for leak testing?
OptionA: Connect a high-pressure nitrogen cylinder directly
OptionB: Circulate controlled hot water through the tubes or use heater blankets to warm the refrigerant
OptionC: Run the compressor at full speed
OptionD: Add extra refrigerant until pressure rises
Explanation: Warming the refrigerant with controlled hot water (or heater blankets) raises its saturation pressure gently and evenly — you can't accidentally overshoot far past 10 psig the way an unregulated nitrogen cylinder can. The margin between 10 psig and the 15 psig rupture disc is small; controlled heat respects it.

QuestionText: Using recovery equipment made after November 15, 1993, a low-pressure appliance must be evacuated to what level?
OptionB: 4 inches Hg vacuum
Explanation: 25 mm Hg ABSOLUTE — very close to a full vacuum — is the low-pressure requirement with current equipment. It's the deepest evacuation level in the 608 rules because the refrigerant is cheap to lose track of in a vacuum machine. Note the unit: millimeters absolute, not inches of vacuum.

QuestionText: 25 mm Hg absolute is approximately equal to what reading on a standard compound gauge?
OptionB: About 25 inches Hg vacuum
Explanation: Atmospheric pressure is about 760 mm Hg absolute. 25 mm absolute means you've removed 735 mm of it — roughly 29 inches Hg of vacuum on a gauge that reads 29.9 at perfect vacuum. The '25 inches' answer is the trap: same number, wrong unit, much shallower vacuum.

QuestionText: In what order is refrigerant removed from a low-pressure chiller?
OptionB: Liquid first, then remove the remaining vapor
OptionC: Oil first, then liquid, then vapor
OptionD: All phases at once through one hose
Explanation: Pump out the liquid first — it holds most of the pounds — then run the recovery unit's compressor to pull out the remaining vapor, which is the slow part of the job on a big machine. Oil is drained separately and heated to reclaim the refrigerant dissolved in it.

QuestionText: During refrigerant recovery (or evacuation) on a chiller, why must water keep circulating through the evaporator and condenser tubes?
OptionA: To keep the pumps from rusting
OptionB: Because evaporating refrigerant chills the tubes — standing water inside them could freeze and rupture the tubes
OptionC: To keep the building cool during service
Explanation: As refrigerant boils off during recovery it absorbs heat from everything around it, including the water tubes. If that water sits still, it can freeze, expand, and split the tubes — a catastrophic repair on a chiller. Moving water can't freeze in place, so circulation stays on.

QuestionText: A significant amount of refrigerant remains dissolved in a chiller's oil after liquid removal. How is it recovered?
OptionA: It can't be — that refrigerant is lost
OptionB: Heat the oil (to roughly 130°F) so the refrigerant boils out and can be recovered as vapor
OptionD: Freeze the oil to separate them
Explanation: Warm oil releases its dissolved refrigerant. Heating the oil to around 130°F drives the refrigerant out as vapor for the recovery unit to capture. Cooling does the opposite — cold oil is a refrigerant sponge. This is also why recovered pressure creeps back up after you stop.

QuestionText: When recharging a low-pressure chiller, why do you charge VAPOR first before introducing liquid?
OptionB: Introducing liquid into the deep vacuum would chill the machine so fast the water in the tubes could freeze — vapor first raises the saturation temperature above freezing (about 36°F)
OptionC: Liquid refrigerant cannot flow into a vacuum
OptionD: It makes the gauges easier to read
Explanation: Liquid flashing into a deep vacuum refrigerates violently — cold enough to freeze the water in the tubes and split them. Charging vapor first brings the machine's pressure up until the saturation temperature is safely above freezing (around 36°F), and only then does liquid go in.

QuestionText: R-123 has a very low allowable exposure limit. What does ASHRAE Standard 15 require for equipment rooms housing R-123 chillers?
OptionB: A refrigerant vapor monitor/sensor with an alarm, plus ventilation — and SCBA available for major leaks
Explanation: ASHRAE 15 calls for refrigerant monitors that alarm before exposure limits are reached, mechanical ventilation, and self-contained breathing apparatus (SCBA) for responding to major leaks. R-123's low exposure limit means you can be in danger at concentrations you can't smell.

QuestionText: Why are room refrigerant sensors and oxygen-deprivation alarms so critical around low-pressure chillers?
OptionA: Because chiller rooms are usually enclosed spaces where leaked refrigerant can displace oxygen or exceed exposure limits before anyone notices
OptionB: Because the sensors are required to run the chiller's compressor
OptionD: They are only installed for insurance discounts
Explanation: Machine rooms are enclosed, often below grade, and refrigerant vapor is heavier than air — a leak can quietly make the air unbreathable or push R-123 past its exposure limit. Alarms buy escape time. R-123 isn't explosive; the danger is what you're breathing.

QuestionText: R-123 was introduced as the interim replacement for which refrigerant in low-pressure chillers?
Explanation: R-123, an HCFC, replaced CFC R-11 in centrifugal chillers when CFC production ended. Same low-pressure machine class, far lower ozone depletion potential. R-12's replacement was R-134a — a different machine class entirely.

QuestionText: New production of R-11 ended with the CFC phase-out in 1996. Where does R-11 for servicing existing chillers come from today?
OptionA: New factories overseas ship it legally into the US
OptionB: Existing stockpiles and reclaimed refrigerant only
OptionC: It is synthesized on-site from R-134a
Explanation: Since production and import ended, every pound of R-11 in circulation is old stock or reclaimed product — which is why recovering it carefully matters economically as well as legally. Owning and servicing existing R-11 machines remains perfectly legal.

QuestionText: During vapor recovery from a low-pressure chiller, what does the recovery unit's water-cooled condenser do?
OptionB: Condenses the recovered refrigerant vapor back into liquid so it can be stored in the receiving drum
Explanation: The recovery compressor pulls vapor from the machine and discharges it into a water-cooled condenser, where it turns back into liquid and drains to the storage drum. Vapor takes up enormous space; condensing it is the only practical way to store the charge.

QuestionText: After evacuating a chiller to 25 mm Hg absolute, you isolate it and the pressure slowly rises. What are the two possible causes you must distinguish between?
OptionB: Refrigerant still boiling out of the oil, or air leaking into the machine
OptionD: Nothing causes pressure rise in a vacuum
Explanation: A rebound after evacuation is either leftover refrigerant off-gassing from the oil (recover more) or an air leak (find and fix it before opening the machine). Watching how far and how fast the pressure climbs — and whether it levels off at the refrigerant's saturation pressure — tells you which one you have.

QuestionText: Before opening a low-pressure chiller for repairs after recovery, what should you do about the deep vacuum inside it?
OptionA: Open it under vacuum — the air rushing in does no harm
OptionB: Break the vacuum with dry nitrogen up to atmospheric pressure, then open it
OptionC: Break the vacuum with compressed shop air
Explanation: Bring the machine to atmospheric pressure with dry nitrogen before opening — controlled, moisture-free, and it prevents a rush of humid room air from soaking the machine's internals. Shop air carries water vapor and oil mist, which is exactly what you're trying to keep out.

QuestionText: A building's R-123 chiller holds 800 pounds of refrigerant and serves comfort cooling. What annual leak rate triggers mandatory repair?
Explanation: Chillers serving building air conditioning are comfort-cooling appliances, and comfort cooling has the strictest trigger: 10% of the full charge per year. The 20% and 30% rates belong to commercial and industrial process refrigeration. At 800 pounds, this machine is squarely inside the 50-pound rule.

QuestionText: Why are HIGH-EFFICIENCY purge units important on low-pressure chillers?
OptionA: They run quieter than standard purges
OptionB: They release far less refrigerant per pound of air removed, cutting both emissions and refrigerant cost
OptionC: They eliminate the need for leak repairs
OptionD: They double the chiller's cooling capacity
Explanation: Every purge cycle expels some refrigerant along with the air. High-efficiency purge units recover nearly all of that refrigerant, losing only a small fraction per pound of air purged — better for the ozone layer, the law, and the owner's wallet. They don't fix leaks; they just manage the air the leaks let in.

QuestionText: Which machine is the classic example of a Type III (low-pressure) appliance?
OptionB: A centrifugal chiller using R-11 or R-123
Explanation: Big centrifugal chillers running R-11 or R-123 are THE low-pressure machines — building-cooling workhorses holding hundreds of pounds of refrigerant in a vacuum. Rooftop units are Type II, chest freezers are Type I small appliances, and car A/C is Section 609.

QuestionText: An idle R-123 chiller sits in a 68°F equipment room. If you crack open a valve on it, what happens?
OptionA: Refrigerant vapor sprays out under pressure
OptionB: Air flows INTO the machine, because R-123's pressure at 68°F is below atmospheric
Explanation: R-123 boils at about 82°F at atmospheric pressure, so at 68°F its saturation pressure is a partial vacuum — lower than the room's pressure. Open a valve and the room pushes air in; the machine doesn't push refrigerant out. This backwards-from-intuition behavior is the heart of Type III.

QuestionText: The 80% maximum fill rule for recovery cylinders applies to low-pressure refrigerants like R-123:
OptionA: No — low-pressure refrigerants can fill to 100%
OptionB: Yes — never fill any recovery cylinder beyond 80% of its capacity
Explanation: The 80% rule is universal: liquid needs room to expand as temperature rises, no matter how low the refrigerant's pressure is at room temperature. A hydrostatically full cylinder of any refrigerant becomes a bomb in a hot truck.

QuestionText: Recovery equipment manufactured BEFORE November 15, 1993 must evacuate a low-pressure appliance to what level?
OptionB: 25 mm Hg absolute
Explanation: Older (pre-November 1993) equipment gets the shallower target: 25 INCHES Hg vacuum. Current equipment must reach 25 MM Hg absolute — nearly a full vacuum. Same digits, different units, very different depths: that pairing is a deliberate exam trap.

QuestionText: A major refrigerant leak occurs in a chiller equipment room. What is the correct first response?
OptionA: Grab a flashlight and go find the leak alone
OptionB: Evacuate the room, ventilate it, and re-enter only with SCBA (never alone)
OptionC: Seal the room doors to contain the vapor
OptionD: Turn off the lights to prevent ignition
Explanation: Get out, get air moving, and go back in only with self-contained breathing apparatus and a partner. Refrigerant displaces oxygen and R-123 exceeds its exposure limit quickly — sealing yourself in with it or searching alone is how fatalities happen. Ignition isn't the primary danger; asphyxiation is.

QuestionText: A technician certified as Type III only may legally work on which of the following?
OptionB: A rooftop R-410A packaged unit
Explanation: Type III covers low-pressure appliances — the R-123 chiller qualifies. The rooftop unit needs Type II and the window unit needs Type I. Working on everything requires Universal certification, which is simply passing all three specialty exams plus Core.
